title: Backend Testing Strategy & Quality Engineering
subtitle: Characterization tests for legacy code, contract testing, flaky test elimination, and CI quality gates in Java microservices

scenarios.question: You've inherited a critical payment processing service with zero test coverage — no unit tests, no integration tests. A product team needs a new feature shipped in 2 weeks. How do you add tests safely without breaking existing behaviour, and how do you write the new feature test-first?
signals: Reaches for characterization (approval) tests to pin existing behaviour before touching code
Identifies seams — places to inject test doubles — without changing production code
Uses Testcontainers for real database in integration tests, not H2
Writes the new feature test-first (red-green-refactor) once a safety net exists
Adds @Transactional(rollback) or per-test dataset cleanup to keep tests isolated
Does NOT refactor legacy code during the feature window — pins first, refactors later
Understands the risk of mocking too early in a system with no domain model clarity

followUps.question: How do you prevent integration tests from becoming flaky when they depend on Testcontainers or external state?
What is contract testing and when should you use it over integration tests with mocks?

scenarios.question: Your CI pipeline runs in 47 minutes. Engineers are skipping it locally and pushing directly. Coverage is dropping. How do you cut pipeline time under 10 minutes without sacrificing coverage or reliability?
signals: Applies the test pyramid: 70% unit, 20% integration, 10% e2e
Uses JUnit 5 tags and Maven profiles to separate fast vs slow tests
Enables parallel test execution via junit-platform.properties
Runs fast tests on every commit, slow tests on PR merges
Caches Maven/Gradle dependencies in CI to skip re-download
Identifies and eliminates the top 5 slowest tests with flamegraphs
Adds a pre-commit hook for the fast test subset

followUps.question: How do you enforce a minimum coverage threshold for new code without failing on the legacy untested code?
followUps.answer:
Use Jacoco's `excludes` combined with a `diffCoverage` tool like Diff Coverage or SonarQube's new-code coverage gate. The goal is: **new code must be tested; existing untested code is allowed but can't grow**.

```xml
<!-- pom.xml: Jacoco rule for new code only -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.jacoco</groupId>
  <artifactId>jacoco-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <configuration>
    <excludes>
      <!-- Exclude legacy packages from coverage gate -->
      <exclude>com/example/legacy/**</exclude>
      <exclude>com/example/generated/**</exclude>
    </excludes>
    <rules>
      <rule>
        <element>PACKAGE</element>
        <limits>
          <limit>
            <counter>LINE</counter>
            <value>COVEREDRATIO</value>
            <minimum>0.80</minimum>  <!-- 80% on non-legacy packages -->
          </limit>
        </limits>
      </rule>
    </rules>
  </configuration>
</plugin>
```

For PR-level enforcement: use **Diff Coverage** (compares coverage only on lines changed in the PR):

```yaml
# CI step
- name: Check new code coverage
  run: |
    pip install diff-cover
    diff-cover target/site/jacoco/jacoco.xml \
      --compare-branch=origin/main \
      --fail-under=80
```

This way, old untested code doesn't block CI, but every line you add must be covered.

title: API Versioning, Evolution & gRPC Migration
subtitle: Backward-compatible API changes, deprecation lifecycle, content negotiation, and deciding when to migrate from REST to gRPC

scenarios.question: Your order creation REST API is consumed by a mobile app on v1.2, a web app on v2.0, and 3 enterprise clients on legacy v1.x. You need to add a mandatory `customerTier` field (GOLD/SILVER/BRONZE) to the order. How do you roll this change out without breaking any client?
signals: Recognizes this as a breaking change (new required field) and applies expand-contract
Adds the field as nullable first (backward-compatible), then enforces after all clients migrate
Chooses a versioning strategy: URL path (/v1/, /v2/), header, or content negotiation
Communicates deprecation with Sunset header and Deprecation header (RFC 8594)
Uses a default value strategy so old clients that omit the field still work
Sets up feature flag or canary to gate the enforcement date

followUps.question: When do you use URL versioning (/v1/, /v2/) versus header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.company.v2+json)?
An enterprise client says they can't migrate from v1 before your sunset date. How do you handle it contractually and technically?
followUps.answer:
**Technically:** extend the sunset date only for that client using a client-specific routing rule:

```yaml
# Spring Cloud Gateway: route enterprise-A to v1-legacy-handler for 6 more months
- id: enterprise_a_v1_bridge
  uri: lb://order-service
  predicates:
    - Header=X-Client-ID, enterprise-client-a
    - Path=/v1/orders/**
  filters:
    - RewritePath=/v1/orders(?<segment>.*), /v2/orders${segment}
    - AddRequestHeader=X-Default-Tier, BRONZE  # inject the default
```

The gateway transparently translates their v1 call to v2 internally — the actual service only runs v2 code.

**Contractually:** issue a formal deprecation notice with a fixed SLA, charge for extended support if it's beyond your standard window, and agree on a written migration plan with milestone checkpoints. Document that the compatibility bridge is not a commitment to keep v1 semantics forever.

scenarios.question: Your team is considering migrating from REST to gRPC for internal service-to-service communication. Three services call each other ~200,000 times per minute. What's your decision framework, and what are the hidden trade-offs you need to surface before making the call?
signals: Understands gRPC uses HTTP/2 multiplexing — one TCP connection, multiple concurrent streams
Knows proto IDL enforces schema contracts and generates client/server stubs automatically
Aware that gRPC is not browser-friendly without gRPC-Web or a transcoding layer
Considers observability: gRPC status codes differ from HTTP, tracing needs gRPC interceptors
Schema evolution trade-off: proto field numbers are stable but adding required fields is breaking
Operational cost: team must learn proto toolchain, protoc compilation in CI
Suggests starting with one non-critical service pair before full migration

followUps.question: How do you handle observability for gRPC services — the gRPC status codes don't map to HTTP 4xx/5xx monitoring out of the box?

title: Microservice Decomposition & Monolith Migration
subtitle: Strangler fig pattern, domain-driven bounded contexts, database-per-service migration, and untangling shared schemas

scenarios.question: Your team maintains an 8-year-old Spring Boot monolith handling user management, order processing, payments, and notifications. It has 200k lines of code, one shared PostgreSQL database, and 40 engineers stepping on each other's toes. The CTO wants a microservices migration over 18 months without any downtime. Where do you start and how do you execute without big-bang rewrites?
signals: Applies strangler fig pattern — migrate incrementally, not big-bang
Uses domain-driven design to identify bounded contexts before writing code
Starts with the least-coupled, lowest-risk domain (typically notifications or reporting)
Routes traffic via API gateway — old monolith and new service run simultaneously
Implements anti-corruption layer to translate between old and new domain models
Knows that database separation comes AFTER the service boundary is stable
Does NOT split services by technical layer (not 'user controller service' vs 'user repo service')
scenarios.answer:
## Strangler Fig Migration: 18-Month Phased Plan

### Step 1 — Domain Mapping (Weeks 1-4, No Code)

```
Bounded Context Analysis:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    MONOLITH (current)                    │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐│
│  │   Users    │  │  Orders  │  │  Payments  + Notif.  ││
│  │  (low      │  │ (high    │  │   (critical, many    ││
│  │  coupling) │  │ coupling │  │    downstream deps)  ││
│  └────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────────────┘│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Extraction priority: Notifications → User Profile → Orders → Payments
(Lowest risk first; payments last — most critical, most entangled)
```

### Step 2 — Introduce API Gateway as the Strangler

```yaml
# Spring Cloud Gateway: all traffic enters here
# Initially routes everything to the monolith
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: monolith-all
          uri: http://monolith:8080
          predicates:
            - Path=/**
```

```yaml
# After notification-service is live: route notifications to new service
routes:
  - id: notification-service
    uri: lb://notification-service   # new service in K8s
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/notifications/**
    filters:
      - StripPrefix=0
  - id: monolith-fallback
    uri: http://monolith:8080
    predicates:
      - Path=/**                     # everything else still goes to monolith
```

Users see one URL. Traffic routing shifts invisibly.

### Step 3 — Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL)

The monolith uses old domain language. The new service uses clean DDD terms.

```java
// Monolith's old model (you can't change this yet)
public class NotifRecord {
    public Long notif_id;
    public String notif_type_code;  // "EML", "SMS", "PUSH"
    public String dest_addr;
    public Integer send_attempts;
}

// New service's clean domain model
public record Notification(
    NotificationId id,
    NotificationType type,       // EMAIL, SMS, PUSH_NOTIFICATION
    RecipientAddress recipient,
    int attemptCount
) {}

// ACL: translates between old and new without polluting the new domain
@Component
public class NotificationAntiCorruptionLayer {
    public Notification fromLegacy(NotifRecord legacy) {
        return new Notification(
            NotificationId.of(legacy.notif_id),
            NotificationType.fromCode(legacy.notif_type_code),
            RecipientAddress.of(legacy.dest_addr),
            legacy.send_attempts != null ? legacy.send_attempts : 0
        );
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Database Separation (After Service Boundary Stable)

```
Phase A (Month 1-2): New service reads/writes the SAME monolith DB table
    → Fast to ship, no data migration risk
    → Anti-corruption layer handles model translation

Phase B (Month 3-4): New service gets its OWN DB schema, dual-write via events
    → Monolith publishes NotificationCreatedEvent to Kafka
    → New service consumes and writes to its own DB
    → Run both in parallel, verify data parity

Phase C (Month 5): Cut-over to new service's DB as authoritative source
    → Monolith stops writing to its notification table
    → Reads from new service via REST/gRPC
    → Old table archived

Phase D (Month 6+): Repeat for next bounded context (User Profile)
```

### Canary Traffic Split at the Gateway

```yaml
# Route 5% of notification traffic to new service; 95% to monolith
# Validate correctness before full cut-over
routes:
  - id: notification-canary
    uri: lb://notification-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/notifications/**
      - Weight=notification-group, 5
  - id: notification-monolith
    uri: http://monolith:8080
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/notifications/**
      - Weight=notification-group, 95
```

followUps.question: How do you choose which bounded context to extract first?
followUps.answer:
Use the **SHIP framework** to score each domain:

| Criterion | Weight | Notifications | Orders | Payments |
|-----------|--------|--------------|--------|----------|
| **S**ize (small = good) | 25% | High | Low | Low |
| **H**azard (low risk = good) | 25% | High | Med | Low |
| **I**solation (few dependencies = good) | 25% | High | Low | Low |
| **P**ain (current pain solved) | 25% | Med | High | High |
| **Score** | | **8/10** | 4/10 | 3/10 |

Notifications wins: small codebase, few outgoing dependencies, if it fails you send a late email — you don't lose money. **Never start with payments** — it's entangled with orders, refunds, ledger entries, and failure means real money lost.

Also check: which team owns the domain? Align service ownership with team boundaries (Conway's Law). Extract services that a single team can own end-to-end.

followUps.question: Two services need the same data — for example, order-service needs customer name from user-service for invoices. Should they share a database or does user-service call order-service?

scenarios.question: You discover that order-service and inventory-service both read and write to the same PostgreSQL schema. Order-service writes to the `inventory` table, and inventory-service reads from the `orders` table. How do you untangle this shared database dependency without downtime?
signals: Immediately stops cross-service writes as highest priority (data ownership violation)
Introduces event-based sync instead of direct table access
Does not create a 'shared services' database — that's the same problem with extra steps
Handles JOIN queries that span tables by denormalizing into local read models
Executes a phased migration: code change first, data migration second, cleanup last
Tests data parity between old and new approach before cutting over

followUps.question: How do you handle a JOIN that currently spans the orders and inventory tables and is needed for a dashboard query?

title: Zero-Downtime Database Schema Migrations
subtitle: Expand-contract pattern, shadow columns, background backfill, and Flyway strategies for migrating large tables without production downtime

scenarios.question: You need to add a non-nullable `customer_tier` enum column (GOLD/SILVER/BRONZE) to the `orders` table which has 500 million rows and receives 50,000 writes per minute in production. A naive ALTER TABLE would lock the table for hours. How do you execute this migration with zero downtime?
signals: Never runs ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN NOT NULL directly on a large table — understands table rewrite cost
Applies expand-contract: add nullable first, backfill in batches, enforce NOT NULL after backfill
Uses CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY for any index on the new column
Implements application code in phases — code ships before NOT NULL enforcement
Knows PostgreSQL 11+ allows adding column with constant default without table rewrite
Monitors backfill progress and runs it at off-peak hours with rate limiting
Understands Flyway repeatable vs versioned migrations

followUps.question: What if the backfill job fails halfway through? How do you make it safe to restart?
How do you use Flyway's expand-contract pattern with separate migration scripts for app deploy vs DB migration?
followUps.answer:
The critical rule: **database migrations must be backward-compatible with the previous app version**. This means you deploy DB migrations before app code, not simultaneously.

```
Flyway naming convention for phased migrations:
  V20250115_1__orders_add_tier_nullable.sql    ← deploy first (expand)
  V20250215_1__orders_enforce_tier_not_null.sql ← deploy after backfill + app upgrade (contract)
```

```yaml
# application.yml — Flyway runs on startup
spring.flyway.enabled: true
spring.flyway.out-of-order: false  # enforce sequential
spring.flyway.validate-on-migrate: true
```

CI/CD sequence:
```
1. Deploy V20250115_1 migration → nullable column added (app v1.x still works)
2. Deploy app v2.0 → starts writing customer_tier on new orders
3. Run backfill job over 4 weeks
4. After backfill verified → deploy V20250215_1 → enforce NOT NULL
5. Old app code (v1.x) that doesn't write customer_tier would now fail — but by this point all pods are on v2.0
```

This gives you a safe rollback window: if v2.0 has bugs, rollback to v1.x — the nullable column is still there and the old code still works.

title: Multi-Tenant Architecture & Data Isolation
subtitle: Cache key namespacing, PostgreSQL Row-Level Security, TenantContext propagation, and preventing cross-tenant data leaks in SaaS systems

scenarios.question: Your SaaS platform serves 10,000 business clients. During a production incident you discover that Company A briefly saw order data belonging to Company B on their dashboard. How do you diagnose the root cause, fix it permanently, and design the system so this class of bug becomes structurally impossible?
signals: Identifies the root cause: missing tenant_id filter in a query, or wrong cache key without tenantId
Applies PostgreSQL Row-Level Security as a DB-level guarantee that survives code bugs
Uses TenantContext (ThreadLocal) propagated through every request to every repository call
Namespaces all Redis keys with tenantId so cache isolation is enforced by key design
Audits all existing queries to find other missing WHERE tenant_id = ? clauses
Adds integration tests that explicitly verify cross-tenant isolation

followUps.question: How does PostgreSQL Row-Level Security interact with HikariCP connection pooling? Won't the `SET app.current_tenant` from one request leak into the next?
Should you use one database per tenant (database-level isolation) or one schema per tenant, or shared tables with tenant_id?
followUps.answer:
Three models, different trade-offs:

| Model | Isolation | Cost | Scale | Use When |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|----------|
| **Shared table + RLS** | App-level + DB policy | Low | High (10k+ tenants) | SaaS with many small tenants |
| **Schema per tenant** | Schema namespace | Medium | Medium (~500 tenants) | Regulated tenants needing schema-level backup/restore |
| **Database per tenant** | Full OS isolation | High | Low (~50 tenants) | Enterprise contracts, data residency requirements |

**Shared tables + RLS** is the right choice for your 10,000-tenant scenario. One PostgreSQL cluster, one schema, RLS enforces isolation. Drawbacks: a misconfigured RLS policy affects all tenants; `VACUUM` contention grows with total rows.

**Schema per tenant** (`CREATE SCHEMA tenant_a; CREATE TABLE tenant_a.orders ...`) gives per-tenant backup/restore with `pg_dump -n tenant_a` but Flyway migrations must run per schema — maintenance multiplies with tenant count.

**Database per tenant** used by Slack (at early scale) — full isolation but you need a tenant-to-database routing layer and connection pool per tenant. Works for high-value enterprise clients where SLAs justify the cost.
